explanatory_strength.rationale: Four quantified thesis pillars tie AWS re-acceleration (+28% Q1-26, 37.7% segment margin), ads and silicon mix, retail productivity, and a capex-normalization path to owner cash. Structural versus cyclical labeling is explicit, and the primary inversion (permanent AI capex overshoot) is directly tested against TTM OCF $148.5B versus purchases ~$147.3B.

evidence_sufficiency.rationale: Q1 2026 and FY2025 SEC extracts anchor sales, segment margins, OCF, and capex, but the sustainable ~$90B capex assumption, diluted share count, and incremental ROIC on AI spend remain judgment. The contract proof uses $7.695B owner earnings from FY2025 OCF while the deep dive normalizes to ~$58.5B ($5.35/sh), and filing_facts_2026-07-20.json is a stale 2003 extract, so low/base/high bridges are not fully reconcilable from the packet alone.

downside_control.rationale: Operating cash flow scale ($148.5B TTM), cash ($86.8B), and equity ($411.1B) bound liquidity stress, and capex-stress FCF of ~$0.11/sh is quantified. Downside is not tight: multiple compression on a franchise priced for AI optionality and a prolonged capex arms race could leave equity returns in low single digits even if OCF stays large.

return_vs_alternatives.rationale: Total synthesis is 3.68% per year at ~$247, Lawrence gate 1.9%, and segment sum-implied 4.1%; all sit well below a ~15% medium-term hurdle. Bull sensitivity to 7.2% (scenario) or 16.2% (assumption-heavy $9/sh normalized owner cash) does not clear the bar on filing-grounded base economics.

claims.claim: Q1 2026 AWS net sales were $37.6 billion (+28% YoY) with segment operating income margin 37.7%, confirming re-acceleration in the profit engine.
TTM operating cash flow was $148.5 billion while TTM capital purchases were ~$147.3 billion, leaving trailing free cash flow of only $1.2 billion and illustrating the AI capex trough.
Lawrence base owner cash of $5.35 per share assumes ~$90 billion sustainable capex against ~$151 billion TTM productive-asset spend, a timing judgment not yet anchored to management guidance.

strongest_counter_explanation: Amazon is rationally priced as a perpetual AI infrastructure compounder: hyperscaler capex competition keeps capital purchases near operating cash flow for years, so reported AWS and ads growth never converts to normalized owner cash per share at a rate that clears a 15% equity return at today's multiple.
most_important_missing_fact: Management-stated sustainable data-center capex run rate and a filing-disclosed timeline for when AI build-out spending moderates enough to lift normalized free cash flow per share toward the $5.35–$9.00/sh bridge.
falsifiers: Two consecutive quarters with operating cash flow growth below 5% YoY while capital purchases stay above $140 billion
AWS revenue growth decelerates below 15% while segment operating margin compresses versus Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud
Normalized owner cash per share fails to inflect above $4 by FY2028 despite capex moderating
specialist_findings: Competitive-advantage mechanics are well mapped (AWS lock-in, Prime, ads attach, custom silicon) and overlap controls treat advertising as embedded rather than double-counted. The owner-earnings reinvestment DCF proof is arithmetically valid but starts from $7.695B FY2025 OCF, which conflicts with the $58.5B normalized bridge in the deep dive; incremental ROIC on AI capex is asserted (12–25% bounds) without a primary-source capital ledger. No management fieldwork this period.
